title: Hibernate Validation Failure Patterns
summary: Guidance for interpreting failed, partial, and timeout validation evidence in checkout-backed Hibernate readiness reports.
guide: Use validation failures as scope evidence. A failed compile or test run is not a useless report; it identifies the first environment or dependency blocker that must be cleared before upgrade execution.

Separate application risk from validation environment risk. Java toolchain mismatch, missing wrappers, blocked repositories, and Docker-dependent tests should be tracked differently from Hibernate API incompatibility.
Keep passing and failing examples together. Buyers need proof that the tool reports both green paths and messy real-world blockers without hiding failure evidence.

risks.name: Java toolchain mismatch
risks.whyItMatters: Older Hibernate examples may require Java 8 or older compiler source levels while the release image runs a modern JDK.
risks.recommendation: Capture the required runtime explicitly and rerun validation with EMP_JAVA_<major>_HOME when a client project depends on an older Java toolchain.

risks.name: Missing Maven or Gradle wrapper files
risks.whyItMatters: Repositories sometimes commit wrapper scripts without wrapper jars, making otherwise valid source impossible to validate repeatably.
risks.recommendation: Prefer system Maven or Gradle when available, or ask the client to restore wrapper metadata before treating validation as authoritative.

risks.name: Test dependency gaps
risks.whyItMatters: A project can compile production code while test compilation fails because old sample tests reference missing JUnit or plugin dependencies.
risks.recommendation: Record compile success separately from test failure and use that distinction in the migration estimate.

risks.name: External service assumptions
risks.whyItMatters: Persistence tests may require Docker, PostgreSQL, MySQL, Elasticsearch, or other services that are not part of a static source checkout.
risks.recommendation: Mark the report as partial validation and ask for the service bootstrap path before promising runtime confidence.

risks.name: Timeout under release budget
risks.whyItMatters: Large tutorial or integration-heavy repositories can exceed the current 120-second public validation budget even when the build might pass later.
risks.recommendation: Treat timeout evidence as a signal to split modules or raise the validation budget for paid client work.

validationNotes: Passing compile plus failing tests is stronger than catalog-only evidence because it proves source and dependency resolution reached the test phase.
A timeout is not the same as an incompatibility. It records the current public evidence budget and should be revisited for client-specific work.
Reports should preserve command, duration, exit code, timeout status, and a short log excerpt so consultants can explain the blocker.
The recommended next action after validation failure is usually environment repair, dependency repair, or module narrowing before migration planning.

benchmarks.name: Hibernate Test Case Templates
benchmarks.note: Compilation passes while Docker-dependent reactive tests fail without a valid Docker environment.

benchmarks.name: Hypersistence Utils
benchmarks.note: Validation records Java toolchain failure evidence for modules requiring an older JDK.

benchmarks.name: High Performance Java Persistence
benchmarks.note: Validation records timeout evidence under the current public check budget.

benchmarks.name: Java Hibernate Hello World
benchmarks.note: Production compilation passes while test compilation records a missing JUnit dependency.

benchmarks.name: Spring JavaConfig Sample
benchmarks.note: Compile and tests pass, giving a small green-path comparison for failed validation examples.

limits: Validation failure evidence does not prove a Hibernate migration is impossible.
Validation failure evidence does not replace maintainer knowledge about required local services.
A passing public validation run does not prove production database behavior.
A timeout under the public budget should not be used as the only estimate for paid remediation work.
